Wall Street Extending Research into Private Companies
data.description:
The growing clout of private companies like OpenAI is causing Wall Street to redraw the boundaries of its equity research business, with banks expanding coverage of private assets to capture their potential impact. Bloomberg's Sridhar Natarajan has more on the story.

Bloomberg videos, provided by MT Newswires

data.title: Nvidia Earnings Help Ease Bubble Fears
data.description:
Stifel Applied Technologies Analyst, Roy Ruben, explains why Nvidia's (NVDA) growth outlook is helping calm fears of an AI bubble. He joins Caroline Hyde and Ed Ludlow on "Bloomberg Tech."

Bloomberg videos, provided by MT Newswires

data.title: Utilities Down, But not by Much, on Safe Haven Demand -- Utilities Roundup
data.description:
Shares of power producers fell, but not by as much as the broad market, as traders rotated into safe havens.

Treasury yields fell, a development that typically supports the price of utility stocks.

The Energy Department is eliminating two of its biggest offices that channeled billions of dollars into clean-energy projects, the Office of Clean Energy Demonstrations and the Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy, another step in President Trump's efforts to block federal support for technologies designed to reduce greenhouse-gas emissions.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-20-25 1745ET

data.title: Communications Services Down as Traders Flee Risk -- Communications Services Roundup
data.description:
Communications-services companies fell sharply as traders retreated from risk.

Verizon Communications started notifying employees Thursday if they are affected by its largest-ever round of layoffs, the company's chief executive said in an all-staff email viewed by The Wall Street Journal.

Warner Bros. Discovery gave back some recent gains as traders awaited confirmation of its plans to sell itself.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-20-25 1745ET

data.title: Tech Down as Nvidia Fails to Quell AI Bubble Fears -- Tech Roundup
data.description:
Shares of technology companies fell after initial ebullience about Nvidia's earnings results quickly faded.

Nvidia was up by more than 5% at one stage after the giant maker of artificial-intelligence enabling chips posted a sharp increase in quarterly revenue and forecast more growth in the fourth quarter. Anxiety about valuations of Nvidia and other AI companies returned to haunt the sector, however, and Nvidia closed more than 3% lower for the session.

"That's a massive move, and a reminder of how brittle positioning has become," said Lorenzo Di Mattia, manager of hedge fund Sibilla Global Fund, who is betting against the Nasdaq. Even if a bubble is bursting, initial corrections at such times are usually followed by recoveries, Di Mattia said.

Shares of Oracle, one of the companies whose aggressive accumulation of debt to fund AI infrastructure deals has rattled markets, fell by almost 7%.

The Roundhill Magnificent Seven exchange-traded fund, a basket of mega-cap tech firms, fell 0.8%, closing on the cusp of correction territory, 10% below its peak in late October.

International Business Machines and Cisco Systems unveiled a plan to collaborate on a network of fault-tolerant quantum computers. Shares of quantum-computing specialists such as Rigetti Computing fell sharply.

Among other high-risk investments, the price of bitcoin tumbled by more than 5%, and is down by more than one third since its highs in October. More than $1 trillion in market capitalization has been wiped out in cryptocurrency markets.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-20-25 1744ET

data.title: Muni Moment: United Airlines Shelves Two Bond Offerings
data.description:
United Airlines Inc. (UAL) shelved two municipal bond issues as the volatility hitting other asset classes makes a rare appearance in the state and local government debt market. Bloomberg's Aashna Shah has more on the story.

Bloomberg videos, provided by MT Newswires

data.title: Financials Down as Global Market Volatility Picks Up -- Financials Roundup
data.description:
Shares of banks and other financial institutions fell as volatility picked up and traders fled risky niches of the market.

Shares of Blue Owl, one of the specialists in private credit, a popular Wall Street vehicle that has funded data-center building and other activities, fell slightly, bringing losses for the year to date to roughly 42%.

Investors were at their peaks during a buoyant morning of trading, the three major U.S. stock indexes were up more than 1.5%, and the biggest gain shed by day's end since April 8, at the height of the tariff scare.

French bank BNP Paribas reassured investors by increasing its target for a capital buffer and launching a new share buyback plan.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-20-25 1742ET

data.title: White House defends Trump calling a female reporter 'piggy'
data.description:
WASHINGTON (Reuters) -The White House on Thursday defended President Donald Trump after he called a female reporter "piggy" as she questioned him about the late convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein, saying the president's remarks reflected his frankness and transparency.

In the exchange aboard Air Force One last week, which has since gone viral, Trump leaned toward the reporter, pointed his finger and said, "Quiet, piggy" as she pressed him about a recently released Epstein email in which the New York financier claimed Trump "knew about the girls."

Asked on Thursday about the incident, White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said American voters re-elected Trump for his frankness and that reporters should appreciate his openness in answering questions.

"He calls out fake news when he sees it and gets frustrated with reporters who spread false information," Leavitt said during a White House briefing, offering no evidence of false information being reported. "But he also provides unprecedented access to the press and answers questions on a near-daily basis."

On Tuesday in the Oval Office, Trump called another female reporter "a terrible person" after she asked visiting Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman about the killing of journalist Jamal Khashoggi and asked Trump why he had not released the Epstein files.

Trump signed legislation on Wednesday ordering the Justice Department to release documents from its long-running investigation into Epstein after initially resisting having the files become public.

The Society of Professional Journalists issued a statement this week condemning Trump's disparaging language toward the reporters, noting his history of using demeaning language to discredit women. The White House declined to comment beyond Leavitt's earlier statement regarding Trump's "piggy" remark.

"Nobody expects presidents to be reporters' biggest fans," SPJ Executive Director Caroline Hendrie said. "But targeting women reporters with humiliating insults should not be tolerated."

(Reporting by Nandita Bose in Washington; Editing by Cynthia Osterman)

By Nandita Bose

data.title: Texas sues Bristol Myers, Sanofi for concealing blood thinner Plavix's alleged ineffectiveness
Consumer Cos Down After Mixed Earnings -- Consumer Roundup
data.description:
Consumer companies fell amid mixed earnings reports.

Walmart shares gained after the world's largest retailer by sales reported robust earnings and raised its annual outlook.

Bath & Body Works plunged after an effort to diversify its product offerings failed to stem a drop in quarterly sales.

Home sales rose to an eight-month high in October, signaling how even a small decline in mortgage rates will attract some buyers back to the market.

The Federal Reserve Bank of Philadelphia said Thursday that its business-activity index for October rose to minus 1.7 from minus 12.8 in October, indicating contraction but at a less sharp pace than last month.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-20-25 1739ET

data.title: Alphabet, Jeff Bezos Back AI Startup Physical Intelligence
data.description:
Physical Intelligence, a startup developing artificial intelligence software to help robots learn a wide range of tasks, has raised $600 million in a new round of funding that values the company at $5.6 billion, according to people with knowledge of the matter. Bloomberg's Shirin Ghaffary has more on the story.

Bloomberg videos, provided by MT Newswires

data.title: Health Care Down, But not by Much, Amid Deal Activity -- Health Care Roundup
data.description:
Health-care companies fell, but not by as much as the broad market, amid deal activity.

Exact Sciences rallied after Abbott Laboratories agreed to buy the cancer diagnostics company for about $21 billion.

Vaccine-maker Moderna told investors that it still thinks it can stop losing money in 2028, and that its financial outlook remains strong, but that it had taken out a $1.5 billion loan to shore up its balance sheet.

A Centers for Disease Control and Prevention webpage that previously made the case that vaccines don't cause autism now says they might, the latest indication that Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy is putting his stamp on the agency.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-20-25 1738ET

data.title: Joby Aviation sues air-taxi rival Archer over trade secrets
Industrials Down Amid Mixed Economic Data -- Industrials Roundup
data.description:
Shares of industrial and transportation companies fell amid mixed economic data.

The U.S. added 119,000 workers to payrolls in September, higher than economists had estimated, according to a long-delayed report from The Labor Department.

"The September jobs report was a mixed bag," said economists at brokerage Bank of America Global Research, in a note to clients. "Stronger-than-expected job growth, stable income growth & higher participation came along with a higher u-rate and downward revisions to job growth in previous months." The BofA economists called it a "'Rorschach' jobs report" that "left the Fed guessing."

The Federal Reserve Bank of Philadelphia said Thursday that its business-activity index for October rose to minus 1.7 from minus 12.8 in October, indicating contraction but at a less sharp pace than last month.

A second large fire broke out Thursday morning at a key aluminum supplier to Ford. A Sept. 16 fire at the Oswego, N.Y., plant, Novelis plant reduced production of F-150 pickups and other models.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-20-25 1738ET

data.title: Trump signs order to remove tariffs from Brazilian beef, coffee
data.description:
(Reuters) -U.S. President Donald Trump signed an executive order on Thursday to lift import duties on Brazilian beef, coffee, and some other agricultural products that were impacted by Trump's 40% tariff announcement in July, the White House said.

The order will affect Brazilian imports to the U.S. on or after November 13 and may require a refund of the duties collected on those goods, according to the text of the order released by the White House.

(Reporting by Ismail Shakil; Editing by Chris Reese)

data.title: Materials Down Amid Risk Aversion, Economic Growth Fears -- Materials Roundup
data.description:
Producers of metals and other raw materials fell amid risk aversion and growth fears.

Gold futures continued a recent decline, closing around $4056 an ounce as the U.S. dollar gained against rivals.

Activist investor Elliott Management has built up a sizable stake in Barrick Mining, putting pressure on the gold and copper producer to consider splitting up its operations.

Copper futures, sometimes called "Dr. Copper" because of their record as a leading indicator of economic activity, shed 1% and are now down 14% from their highs earlier in the year.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-20-25 1736ET

data.title: Energy Down on Risk Aversion -- Energy Roundup
data.description:
Shares of energy companies fell as a wave of risk aversion continued to sweep through global markets.

Unexpectedly strong jobs growth in September and earnings from Nvidia failed to assuage investor fears about economic and market weakness.

U.S. sanctions are forcing the sale of Russia's flagship oil company in Serbia, with the Abu Dhabi National Oil Company, based in the United Arab Emirates, in talks to buy Russia's stake in the energy company NIS.

Fighting has flared up again in Gaza and Lebanon despite cease-fires on both fronts, the latest sign of the challenges in bringing sustained peace to the Middle East.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-20-25 1736ET

Nike Raises Quarterly Dividend by 2.5%
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall


Nike is raising its quarterly dividend by 2.5% to 41 cents a share, from 40 cents.

The dividend is payable Jan. 2 to shareholders of record at the close of business Dec. 1, Nike said.

The new payout, equivalent to $1.64 a year, represents an annual yield of about 2.7% based on Thursday's closing price of $61.43.

This is the 24th consecutive year that Nike has raised its quarterly dividend, the company said.


Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

Citigroup Names Luchetti as CFO; To Reorganize U.S. Personal Banking -- Update
data.description:
By Kelly Cloonan


Citigroup appointed a new chief financial officer and is also reorganizing its U.S. personal banking business, including making its U.S. consumer cards business a standalone unit.

The New York bank on Thursday named Gonzalo Luchetti as its next CFO, promoting an internal hire who has been with the company since 2006.

Luchetti will succeed Mark Mason in early March, Citi said. Luchetti has led Citi's U.S. personal banking division since 2021, and previously held a variety of finance and strategy roles at the bank.

Mason has been CFO since 2019, joined Citi in 2001. Upon transitioning out of Citi's top finance role, he will become executive vice chair of Citi and senior executive adviser to Chief Executive Jane Fraser. He intends to pursue leadership opportunities outside of Citi by the end of next year, the company said.

As part of the changes to its U.S. personal banking business, Citi will create a new standalone unit, U.S. Consumer Cards, which will include both its Branded Cards and Retail Services businesses. The unit, one of Citi's five core businesses, will be led by Pam Habner, who has been head of branded cards and lending at Citi since 2020. She will also become a member of the firm's executive management team.

Other parts of the U.S. personal banking business, including retail banking and U.S. Citigold, will be folded into its wealth business. Various relationship tiers under that unit -- U.S. Everyday Banking, Citi Priority, Citigold and Citigold Private Client -- will fall under the leadership of Kate Luft, who will become head of U.S. retail banking and Citigold.

The bank said that integrating these businesses under wealth will help it realize more synergies across relationship tiers, unify management of its consumer deposit taking franchises and allow for better strategic decision making about investment priorities and in other areas.


Write to Kelly Cloonan at kelly.cloonan@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-20-25 1715ET
